coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO hybrid nanostructured materials as quantum-dots sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Rangel-Mendez, Jose R.
Matos, Juan
Cházaro-Ruiz, Luis F.
González-Castillo, Ana C.
Barrios-Yáñez, Guillermo
coredata.dc:description: The microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO hybrid materials was performed. Saccharose, titanium isopropoxide and zinc acetate were used as organic and inorganic sources for the synthesis. The influence of temperature and reaction time on the textural and optoelectronic properties of the hybrid materials was verified. Carbon quantum-dots of TiO2 and ZnO nanostructured spheres were obtained in a second pot by controlled calcination steps of the precursor hybrid materials. A carefully characterization by adsorption-desorption N2 isotherms, XRD, XPS, SEM, UV–vis/DR and electro- and photo-electrochemistry properties of the carbon quantum-dots TiO2 and ZnO spheres was performed. The photoelectrochemical activity of TiO2-C and ZnO-C films proved to be dependent on the conditions of synthesis. It was found a red-shift in the energy band gap of the semiconductors with values of 3.02eV and 3.13eV for the TiO2-C and ZnO-C, respectively, clearly lower than those on bare semiconductors, which is associated with the C-doping effect. From the photo-electrochemistry characterization of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO films can be concluded that the present materials have potential applications as photoelectrodes for quantum-dots sensitized solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-doped materials
Microwave-synthesis
TiO2
ZnO
Quantum-dots
Solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO
Voltammetric characterization of FTO/TiO2-C electrodes in the dark and under light irradiation
Voltammetric characterization of FTO/ZnO-C electrodes in the dark and upon light irradiation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for the microwave-assisted (MWA) synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO.
SEM micrographs of TiO2-C samples. TiO2-C-120-30 (A), TiO2-C-120-150 (B), TiO2-C-150-30 (C) and TiO2-C-150-150 (D).
SEM micrographs of ZnO-C samples. ZnO-C-120-30 (A), ZnO-C-120-150 (B), ZnO-C-150-30 (C) and ZnO-C-150-150 (D).

Pore size distribution of calcined samples. Plot of incremental pore volume vs. pore width for sample (A) TiO2-C and (C) ZnO-C, and plot of cumulative pore volume vs. pore width for sample (B) TiO2-C and (D) ZnO-C.
XRD patterns of calcined TiO2-C (A) and ZnO-C (B) samples.
UV–vis/DR spectra plotted as the Kubelka-Munk function of the reflectance F(R) of TiO2-C (A) and ZnO-C (B).
XPS full spectrum analysis of TiO2-C-150-30 (A) and ZnO-C-150-30 (C). High-resolution XPS spectra in the C 1s peaks of TiO2-C-150-30 (B) and ZnO-C-150-30 (D).

Cyclic voltammetry curves (υ=20mVs−1) obtained in the dark and under 365nm excitation, in 1M LiClO4 EtOH solution, for TiO2-C films previously prepared under different conditions: A) TiO2-C-150-150, B) TiO2-C-150-30 and C) TiO2-C-120-150.

Cyclic voltammetry curves (υ=20mVs−1) obtained in the dark and under 365nm excitation, in 1M LiClO4 EtOH solution, for ZnO-C films previously prepared under different conditions: A) ZnO-C-150-30, B) ZnO-C-150-150 and C) ZnO-C-120-150.

Open circuit potential variation curves, E(V) vs. time. (A): TiO2-C and (B): ZnO-C films of the samples prepared under different conditions of temperature and time of microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis.
Spheres and particle size of ZnO-C −C materials.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Not estimated because the samples do not have a regular shape.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Textural properties of TiO2-C and ZnO-C.
Crystalline phase composition of TiO2C materials.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Obtained from the quantification of all crystalline phases detected in the XRD patterns.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of the atomic surface composition (At wt.%) obtained from XPS spectra.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO hybrid nanostructured materials as quantum-dots sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental Science Division, IPICYT, Camino a La Presa de San José 2055, Col. Lomas 4a sección, San Luis Potosí, 78216, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Camino a La Presa de San José 2055

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomaterials Department, Technological Development Unit (UDT),University of Concepcion, Av. Cordillera, 2634, Parque Industrial Coronel, Coronel, Bio Bio, Chile

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO hybrid materials was performed. Saccharose, titanium isopropoxide and zinc acetate were used as organic and inorganic sources for the synthesis. The influence of temperature and reaction time on the textural and optoelectronic properties of the hybrid materials was verified. Carbon quantum-dots of TiO2 and ZnO nanostructured spheres were obtained in a second pot by controlled calcination steps of the precursor hybrid materials. A carefully characterization by adsorption-desorption N2 isotherms, XRD, XPS, SEM, UV–vis/DR and electro- and photo-electrochemistry properties of the carbon quantum-dots TiO2 and ZnO spheres was performed. The photoelectrochemical activity of TiO2-C and ZnO-C films proved to be dependent on the conditions of synthesis. It was found a red-shift in the energy band gap of the semiconductors with values of 3.02eV and 3.13eV for the TiO2-C and ZnO-C, respectively, clearly lower than those on bare semiconductors, which is associated with the C-doping effect. From the photo-electrochemistry characterization of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO films can be concluded that the present materials have potential applications as photoelectrodes for quantum-dots sensitized solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Climate change, associated with global warming is the result of excessive use of fossil fuels. This has led us to evaluate renewable energy sources such as solar or photovoltaic cells. Sunlight is an attractive energy source, clean, and renewable. It has been estimated that by only covering 1% of the earth's surface, photovoltaic energy will supply the global demand on energy [1], nowadays around 20 TW. An exponential growth in the installed capacity of thermo-solar and photovoltaic energy from 1400MW in 2007 to more than 136,000MW at 2014 [2] has been registered. This growth is linked to the price of electric energy produced from solar energy that has fallen and will continue to fall in the following years due to an increased production of photovoltaic systems with high efficiency with more solar thin-film domiciliary facilities.

Photovoltaic (PV) cell technology can be classified in first, second and third generation. The former occupies 85% of the market and is based on single or multiple crystals of silicon with a conversion efficiency from solar to electric energy between 15 and 30%. However, silicon solar cells have some disadvantages such as high manufacturing cost and the emission of environmentally harmful chemicals during manufacture [3]. The second and third generation are based on thin layers of multiple inorganic oxides or in rare-earth’s such as lanthanides-based materials which commercially have an efficiency less than 15% and beside of the high cost, some components of these solar cells such as CdTe or GaAs have a high degree of toxicity [4]. In short, 2nd and 3rd generation commercial solar cells have some general disadvantages such as a high fabrication cost and emission of environmentally unfriendly chemicals during fabrication. In addition, these commercial solar cells have also an important limitation in one of the most important parameters as is the light harvesting efficiency (LHE) because the low scattering of light [5,6].

It can be said that the report on a dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) in 1991 by O’Regan and Grätzel [7] is the starting point of the last generation’ solar cells. The photosensitization effect of a dye adsorbed on nanocrystalline Titania or the co-sensitization by inorganic compounds have been studied extensively because of their high-power conversion efficiency (>10%) and low production costs reported in several works [8–21]. The photosensitization of semiconductors such as TiO2 and ZnO requires a visible light-induced electron or hole injection into conduction band or valence band, respectively [16] which is strongly affected by the energy band gap (Ebg) of the semiconductor and by the chemical environment due to the presence of doping agents in the framework of TiO2 or ZnO [21–36]. For example, it has been reported that carbon- or nitrogen-doped semiconductors induced a red-shift in the Ebg and accordingly an increase in the photoefficiency of TiO2-based photoelectrodes for solar cells [17,22,25–30]. It has been also reported [29] an enhancement in the photocurrent density of TiO2-based photoelectrodes in presence of CdS and PbS nanoparticles. More recently, an increase in the photoelectrochemical response of TiO2 nanowires decorated with Ag nanoparticles due to the surface plasmon-driven of the noble metal has been also reported [30]. In addition, remarkably effects of CdS, graphene, and Cu2O upon the optical properties of ZnO-bases thin films have been reported for CdS/ZnO/graphene composites [31], and ZnO/Cu2O thin films [32]. The influences of the water content and solvent as well as the addition of ammonia during the solvothermal synthesis upon the optical properties and photocurrent response of ZnO-based thin films have been also reported [33,34].

The light harvesting efficiency (LHE) is the driven force for an enhanced performance of a solar cell. However, mainly due to the enormous difficulty of synthesizing simultaneously microspheres composed by SiO2 or Lanthanides-based solar cells with mesoporous texture, another promising candidate of solar energy-responsive materials concerns to C-doped TiO2 hollow spheres because these materials have proved to enhance the LHE [17–21]. Our group have reported [17,24] the synthesis of C-doped TiO2 hollow spheres with mesoporous texture and with a TiO2 crystalline framework mainly consisted of anatase phase. The C composition was tailored between 1.2 up to 1.5wt% by a controlled calcination of the biomass-derived carbon source. These hybrid C-doped TiO2 spheres showed a high photoactivity in the visible range for the degradation of methylene blue [17], attributed to a red-shift in the energy band gap of TiO2 (lower than commercial TiO2-P25, ca. 3.22eV), consequence of the carbon doping. This material can be considered as a carbon quantum-dot sensitized solar cell (QDSSC) [24]. In addition, a greater absorption of photons in visible light (approximately 420nm) has been reported [23] up to 23% in comparison with TiO2-P25, indicating that the control of the internal space (texture of the mesopores) of the C-doped TiO2 spheres plays an important role upon the scattering of light and therefore on the LHE of the photoelectrode. This phenomenon has been also reported by other groups [18,20].

In this sense, the design of novel nanomaterials and composites plays a major role in the future of the photovoltaic (PV) cell technology. Despite many works have reported important enhancements in the optoelectronic response of photoactive semiconductors, many of these materials have been prepared by using expensive or non-commercial methodologies such as electron beam evaporation [28], pulsed laser deposition [14], vapor deposition [15], high temperature annealing [37], among others. In contrast, few studies have aimed at verifying the influence of quantum carbon points on solar cells based on TiO2 using economical and environmentally friendly synthesis methods such as sol-gel, hydro/solvothermal [22,24,36] and even very few papers have reported on microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis to obtain C-doped TiO2-based mesoporous materials [23]. Microwave-assisted processes permit to reduce the time of reaction and more importantly a better control of the morphology and texture of the spheres because the uniformity of energy supplied compared to a conventional convection heat in an oven.

In this sense, a systematic study of the influence of the temperature and time of reaction in the microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO hybrid spheres was performed. Saccharose was used as the carbon source because is a mainly component of many biomass residues. Quantum-dots sensitized TiO2 and ZnO spheres were obtained by controlled the calcination steps of the precursor hybrid materials. The texture, the crystalline framework, and the surface chemistry of the C-doped TiO2 and ZnO spheres were correlated with their optical (UV–vis spectra) and electrochemistry properties.

Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (97%), saccharose (BioXtra 99.5%) and zinc acetate (99.99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and absolute ethanol (99.99%) from Fermont. All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Commercial TiO2 P25 from Evonik (ex-Degussa) and ZnO from Degussa, were used for comparative purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: The C-doped materials were synthetized using a microwave oven (Monowave 400-Anton Paar). Fig. 1 shows the schematic steps of the microwave-assisted synthesis (MWA). In a typical synthesis, 0.5g of titanium(IV) isopropoxide and 0.5g of saccharose were dissolved in 9mL of absolute ethanol. The resulting solution was poured into a borosilicate glass vial and exposed to microwave irradiation at a selected reaction temperature (120, 135 and 150°C) during a specified time (30 and 150min for each temperature) with a magnetic stirring rate of 600rpm. Then, the solution was fast cooled to 55°C using an air compressor. The resulting solid was filtered and washed several times with ethanol and the final powder was dried at 80°C for 4h in an oven, followed by a calcination process at 550°C for 5h.

All samples were denoted as TiO2-C followed with the assigned temperature and time, ca. TiO2-C-120-30 means C-doped TiO2 material prepared at 120°C by 30min and submitted to calcination at 550°C for 5h.

For the synthesis of C-doped ZnO-based materials, 0.5g of saccharose and 0.5g of zinc acetate were dissolved in 9mL of ethanol. The synthesis procedure was the same as for TiO2-C. In short, the samples were exposed to microwave irradiation, then filtered, dried and finally calcined at 550°C for 5h. The temperature and time of synthesis were the same as those chosen for TiO2-C samples. Each sample was denoted in the same way as TiO2-C with the initial name ZnO-C. The reaction progress for both synthesis was visually followed by color changes with a standard integrated camera attached to the Monowave 400 microwave oven.

Chemical composition of the samples was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a PHI 5000 VERSAPROBE II spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al-Kα X-ray beam (1486.6eV). The beam voltage was 15kV, with a power of 25W and spot beam size of 100μm. Data treatment was performed by using AAnalyzer® software. This is a peak-fitting program for photoemission data which is useful for the deconvolution of XPS peaks. All XPS spectra are referenced to 284.5eV corresponding to C 1s region and from the integration of all the peaks, the atomic surface composition (At wt.%) was determined for C, O, Ti, and Zn atoms. From these values, the ratios Ti/C, O/C, Zn/C, and O/Zn, were estimated to verify the stoichiometric of the oxides and the degree of interaction between C and O with metallic atoms. Sample morphology was verified with ESEM (Environmental Scanning Electron Microscopy) micrographs taken in a FEI QUANTA 250-FEG microscope equipped with a Field Emission Gun. The microscope operates at 20kV (TiO2-C) and 25kV (ZnO-C). Identification of the crystalline phases of TiO2 and ZnO was done from the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns obtained in a SmartLab RIGAKU diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ=1.5418Å). The scanning range was from 20° to 80° for TiO2-C and 20° to 100° for ZnO-C. The scanning speed was 5°min−1 with a scanning step of 0.01°. Diffuse reflectance UV–vis absorption spectra (DR/UV–vis) were recorded on an Agilent Cary 6000i UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer, equipped with an integrating sphere. Finally, BET surface area and pore size distribution were determined by adsorption-desorption N2 isotherms at 77K using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020.

The working electrodes were fabricated using 1.0×1.0cm cut glasses of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO), which were exhaustively washed with isopropanol and sonicated for 30min before the deposition of TiO2-C or ZnO-C [38]. A paste was obtained by mixing 50mg of powders dispersed in a solution of terpineol-ethylcellulose in acetone (6wt% ethylcellulose referred to terpineol weight) by stirring the mixture for 48h [39]. The FTO electrode was coated with a layer of paste using the doctor blade technique between two parallel strips of adhesive tape. Finally, the film on the glass was sintered at 450°C for 30min. The area of the electrode was 0.3cm2.

Electrochemical tests were performed in a conventional three-electrode quartz cell. A Ag/AgCl/NaCl 3M system was used as the reference electrode. The counter electrode was a platinum mesh (99.999% Alfa Aesar). The 1M LiClO4 ethanol solution was prepared using Milli-Q water (18.2MΩcm), LiClO4 (Sigma-Aldrich, ≤95% purity), and prior to each test, the solution was bubbled with argon for 15min. Photoelectrochemical tests were performed with a potentiostat/galvanostat model VSP SAS Bio-Logic controlled by EC-Lab software V 10.23. The FTO/TiO2-C or FTO/ZnO-C electrodes were placed on one side of the cell to allow the illumination from its rear part, i.e. through either FTO/TiO2-C or FTO/ZnO-C interfaces; the electric contact was made using a Cu wire. The illumination was done with a UV Pen Ray (Hg) lamp (12.2W/m2) into a quartz tube with an emission of 365nm.
Fig. 2 shows the SEM micrographs of calcined TiO2-C samples. Samples are composed by spherical particles with several micrometer sizes with a mean particle size of around 0.30μm as shown in Table 1 .

These samples present a smaller size respect to the samples before calcination (Fig. S1, Supplementary material), which agrees with the pyrolysis of carbon composition during calcination but remaining the spherical shape. It is important to remark the high mechanical and thermal stability of the TiO2-C samples because their morphology did not change after calcination. In addition, it may be seen that some spheres are still connected to each other indicating that the coalescence mechanism was the driving force in the formation of these materials [40]. The interface between spheres is showed by the white arrows inset the figures. Our group have reported that this coalescence is the result of void growth in such a porous solid [41] and therefore, it can be suggested that TiO2-C is a nanostructured material.

Contrary to TiO2-C materials, the ZnO-C samples showed an important dependence on the temperature and time of reaction. The ZnO-C-120-30 sample (Fig. 3 A) showed some remarkable differences from the others calcined samples of ZnO-C.

This sample presented a spiral shape (inset in Fig. 3A), which is formed by nanoparticles. It is important to remark that the ZnO-C-120-30 sample was not evaluated with other characterization techniques, because the yield after calcination was very small (ca. 0.2wt%). By contrast, Fig. 3B shows that ZnO-C-120-150 sample is composed by aggregates of nanoparticles without regular shape. Both samples have a similar mean nanoparticle size, as shown in Table 2 . The ZnO-C-150-30 (Fig. 3C) and ZnO-C-150-150 (Fig. 3D) samples, are clearly nanostructured by aggregation of nanoparticles.

Table 2 shows a summary of the size of the ZnO-C spheres and their constituent particles. In addition, ZnO-C samples showed empty spaces within the nanoparticles and therefore, it can be concluded that these ZnO-C samples are hollow spheres. Contrary to the TiO2-C samples, all ZnO-C samples presented changes in their morphology with respect to the samples before the calcination process (Fig. S2, Supplementary material).
The pore size distribution of the calcined TiO2-C and ZnO-C samples is presented in Fig. 4 , and a summary of the textural properties is shown in Table 3 .

It can be seen from Fig. 4A and B that most of TiO2-C samples are mainly composed by macropores, except for the TiO2-C-150-150 sample which showed a clear contribution of mesopores. Accordingly, the BET surface area of these samples is very low, between 5 and 6m2/g, except for the TiO2-C-150-150 sample with the highest BET surface of 10m2/g, due to the presence of mesopores. It is important to point out that due to experimental limitations in the microwave equipment used for the synthesis of the present materials, the maximum temperature of reaction was fixed at 150°C which is lower than that to the corresponding thermal degradation of saccharose [17,24,41]. So, low BET surface areas were expected. In addition, these low surface areas can be consequence of sintering of nanoparticles. It can be suggested that precursor materials obtained by the MWA synthesis are not totally stable so the sintering of the nanoparticles in the samples during the calcination is easily promoted. It can be inferred because the present BET surface areas were clearly lower than those reported for spherical TiO2-C materials obtained by solvothermal synthesis following calcination [24].

On the other hand, ZnO-C samples (Fig. 4C and D) showed a high contribution of macropores than TiO2-C samples. Accordingly, these samples presented smaller BET surface areas than TiO2-C samples, between 4 and 5m2/g independently of the synthesis conditions. Despite these very low surface area values, it can be concluded that MWA is a very useful, cheap and eco-friendly methodology to tailor a mesopore textural framework. For example, it is important to remark that TiO2-C and ZnO-C samples have an important contribution of mesopores of about 48% and 31% in average, respectively. These values indicate that despite the low values of surface area, the present samples could be effective used as a photoelectrode for quantum-dot sensitized solar cells because their high mesopore contribution could favor the scattering of photons promoting an increase in the LHE.

Fig. 5 A illustrates the XRD patterns of the obtained TiO2-C calcined samples. All samples are composed by a mixture of anatase and rutile phases. The diffraction peaks observed at 2θ=25.3, 37.8, 48.1, 55.1 and 62.7, can be indexed as (101), (004), (200), (211) and (118) crystal planes of anatase phase (PDF card 01-083-1286). The peaks observed at 2θ=27.4, 36.1, 41.2, 54.3 and 69.0, can be indexed as (110), (101), (111), (211) and (301) crystal planes of rutile phase (PDF card 01-084-1283). The sharp peaks are indicative of large crystalline size in agreement with the very low BET surface areas (Table 3) and with the micrometric size observed in the SEM images from Fig. 2 summarized in Table 1. Considering all the anatase and rutile peaks detected, the phase proportion in each sample was determined using the PowderCell Program. It can be seen from Table 4 , that the proportion of anatase and rutile phase can be tailored by controlling the synthesis parameters.

As a general trend, values in Table 4 showed that the higher the temperature and the high the time of synthesis, a higher proportion of anatase phase is observed. However, it is considered that bulk anatase phase begin to transform irreversibly to rutile in air at about 600°C but the presence of impurities or dopants such as carbon, promotes a phase transition at lower temperature due to the high reduction potential of carbon [42]. In the present experimental conditions, an excess of carbon composition in the hybrid C-doped TiO2 materials is expected and therefore the formation of high rutile proportion is promoted during the calcination step. We do believe this result is very promising because it has been reported that a mixture of anatase and rutile phases contributes largely to a higher photo efficiency of TiO2-based materials [43].

On the other hand, the XRD patterns of ZnO-C calcined samples are shown in Fig. 5B. All peaks found in ZnO-C in the XRD pattern were compared against the ZnO (PDF card 00-036-1451). The most significant diffraction peaks at 2θ=31.7, 34.4, 36.2, 47.5, 56.6, 62.8, 68.0 and 69.1, can be indexed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (112) and (201) crystal planes of ZnO, and it can be concluded that the ZnO obtained corresponds to the zincite structure. All the XRD patterns of the present ZnO-C samples showed the same diffraction peaks and the shape of peaks were similar regardless of the synthesis conditions. The XRD peaks in ZnO-C samples are clearly sharper than those observed in the TiO2-C samples suggesting larger crystalline size in agreement with the very low BET surface areas (Table 3) and with the micrometer size observed in the SEM images from Fig. 3 and summarized in Table 2.

The DR/UV–vis spectra plotted as the Kubelka-Munk function of the reflectance R versus the energy of exciting light for the calcined TiO2-C and ZnO-C samples are shown in Fig. 6 . Commercial TiO2-P25 and ZnO are included for comparative purposes. The DR/UV-vis spectra showed the typical broad absorption band, ascribed to the charge transfer process from O2− to Ti4+ or O2− to Zn2+ responsible for the band-gap of the semiconductors. It must be point out the different trends of absorption of photons between the standard TiO2 and ZnO. Absorption of photons in TiO2 monotonically decreases at wavelength higher than 200nm while for the case of commercial ZnO an increase of absorption of photons is observed up to about 350nm and then monotonically decreases. This is because TiO2 is an indirect semiconductor while ZnO is a direct one [44–46].

However, it can be observed a remarkable change in the DR/UV-vis spectra for the calcined TiO2-C samples with a broad absorbance in the visible region, which suggest they reflect significantly less light than the bare TiO2-P25. This is due to the carbon doping within the framework of TiO2 indicating the formation of quantum-dots. This result is very promising because an enhanced scattering of photons within the sphere was achieved and accordingly, a better LHE parameter is expected.

It can be seen in Fig. 6A that the calcined TiO2-C samples presented a red-shift to the visible range in comparison with the P25-TiO2. The band gap energies of the samples were estimated from the tangent lines in the plots of the modified Kubelka-Munk function as reported elsewhere [45]. A similar trend but not so marked is observed in Fig. 6B where ZnO-C samples showed a red-shift in comparison to the commercial ZnO. A summary of the energy band gaps (Ebg) estimated from the tangent line in the plots of Kubelka-Munk function are disclosed in Table 5 (TiO2-C) and Table 6 (ZnO-C).

According to the band gap energies in Table 5, the modification of synthesis parameters (temperature and time) do not make a remarkable change in the energy band gaps of TiO2-C samples (3.02–3.05eV). However, these values are clearly lower than that obtained for the commercial P25-TiO2 (ca. 3.26eV) which is in good agreement with values reported for Anatase TiO2 ca. 3.2eV [1,11,35]. In consequence, the calcined TiO2-C spheres are clearly more photoactive under visible light irradiation than bare TiO2. It is important to remark, that the TiO2-C-150-150 and TiO2-C-150-130 samples showed the highest red-shift with an energy band gap of about 3.02eV and 3.04eV, respectively. These samples showed the highest mesoporous contribution (39–75%, Table 3) suggesting its potential to be used as photoelectrodes due to their mesopore framework would promotes a higher LHE parameter.

Contrary to TiO2-based samples, it can be seen from the DR/UV-vis spectra of the calcined ZnO-C samples (Fig. 6B) a lightly red-shift to the visible-light range (3.13–3.17eV) in comparison with energy band gap ca. 3.26eV for the commercial ZnO which is very similar to other commercial ZnO previously reported [45]. As in the case of TiO2-C samples, the modification of synthesis parameters does not change remarkably the band gap energies.

Despite of the difference between the band gaps of the ZnO and ZnO-C is ca. 0.10–0.13eV (Table 6), we do believe this result is a promising one. It can be remarked that ZnO-C-150-30 showed the highest red-shift (3.13eV) in comparison to commercial ZnO, and this sample also showed the highest mesopore contribution (45%, Table 3). This result indicates that tailoring the porosity characteristics by microwave-assisted synthesis is a promising tool for the preparation of C-doped mesopore photoactive semiconductors.

XPS is a useful technique to quantify the carbon composition and to verify the formation of metal-carbon boning within the crystalline framework of TiO2 [24] or ZnO [46]. Fig. 7 shows the XPS full spectrum analysis and the high-resolution XPS spectra in the C 1s region for two selected calcined samples, TiO2-C-150-30 (Fig. 7A and B) and ZnO-C-150-30 (Fig. 7C and D). Table 7 shows a summary of the atomic surface composition (At. Wt.%) obtained from the XPS spectra.

Both full spectra (Fig. 7A and C) showed an important composition of O, Ti and Zn atoms in TiO2–C-150-30 (Fig. 7A) and ZnO-C-150-30 (Fig. 7C) samples. Carbon composition is also observed in both samples. High resolution XPS spectra in the C 1s region are shown in Fig. 7B (TiO2-C-150-30) and Fig. 7D (ZnO-C-150-30). In both spectra, three peaks were detected. The peak at about 284.6eV corresponds to elemental graphitic carbon (CC bonds) [47] while the peaks at around 285.8eV and 288.7eV can be attributed to aliphatic CO and CO bonds [48] commonly related to carbonyl species adsorbed on the surface [49].

Although the formation of metallic carbides has been reported for C-doped samples [9,24,35], these were not observed in the present samples. Accordingly, the CO peak can be due to the formation of COTi bonds where carbon atoms are bonded to the skeletal framework of the semiconductor indicating the presence of carbon quantum-dots. This agrees with the important carbon surface composition of ca. 25.8% and 28.8% for TiO2-C-150-30 and ZnO-C-150-30 samples (Table 7). This high carbon surface composition is in good agreement with the DR/UV-vis trends observed in Fig. 6 and with the red-shift observed for both samples.

It can be seen from Table 7 that the O/Ti and O/Zn ratios are higher than 2 or much higher than 1, which are the stoichiometric proportions for TiO2 and ZnO, respectively. This is indicative of an important contribution of the oxygen groups associated with surface carbon atoms, which agrees with the CO and CO peaks detected in the XPS spectra. This is a very important issue reported by several groups [11,12,17,44,46,50–53] because the presence of heteroatoms influences the optoelectronic properties of TiO2 as well as the own carbon-based materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric characterization of FTO/TiO2-C electrodes in the dark and under light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry tests (CVs) in the dark and under illumination were performed as a first approach to understand the electrochemical properties of the present C-doped TiO2. The TiO2-C-120-150, TiO2-C-150-30 and TiO2-C-150-150 were selected and films of these materials were studied in 1M LiClO4 ethanol solution within a potential window from 1.5 to −1.2V vs. Ag/AgCl/NaCl 3M, at a potential scan rate, υ, of 20mVs−1. The voltammetry responses are shown in Fig. 8 A, B and C for TiO2-C-120-150, TiO2-C-150-30 and TiO2-C-150-150, respectively. The CV of FTO/1M LiClO4 interface (blank test) showed a negligible current in the potential range here considered (results not shown), thus the observed currents on CVs (Fig. 8) are due to the TiO2-C films.

The potential scan started in cathodic direction from open circuit potential (EOCP). The cathodic current started to increase at potentials close to −0.5V with the TiO2-C-150-150 film forming a broad peak at −1.0V. The cathodic peak was displaced towards more positive potential at −0.75 and −0.65V for TiO2-C-120-150 and TiO2-C-150-30 films, respectively. After this, a fast increase in the current was observed until achieving Eλ=−1.2V where the potential scan was reversed with a fast decrease of the cathodic current and formation of anodic peaks around −1.15V. Then, the current decreases again and became very small until an anodic peak Ep appeared at potential ca. 0.88V, at which oxygen evolution reaction at the electrode started. Finally, when a potential of 1.5V was reached and the scan was reversed, the current became very small again until the starting point of the scan is reached.

The cathodic peak of the CVs is related to the filling of electrons in surface states within the gap energy between TiO2 bands. The increase of cathodic currents at Eλ =−1.2V and the anodic peak at −1.15V can be associated with the adsorption and desorption of protons, respectively [54].

The variation of the voltammetric currents and the onset potential in the reduction region varied for each TiO2-C sample. The variation of the peak potential in which the cathodic peak appears, indicates a change in the energy distribution of the grain boundary surface states inside the films, i.e. the defects formed by the contact of the TiO2 particles [55,56], which clearly have changed with the synthesis conditions. The cathodic peak of the TiO2-C-150-150 is displaced towards more negative potential than the others. This change could be associated to the reduction of defects in the grain boundaries as this sample showed an increase of its porosity (SBET =10m2 g−1) in contrast to the others TiO2-based materials. In the presence of illumination, all the samples presented a current increase in their voltammetric responses, higher than in the dark, reaching an anodic current plateau in accordance with the semiconductor properties of TiO2, as previously reported for analogous samples prepared by solvothermal synthesis [24]. Also, it is important to remark that the cathodic photocurrents appeared at more positive potentials than the flat-band potential of single-crystal n–TiO2 electrodes, which is attributed to a more efficient electron transfer from the conduction band of the TiO2 nanoparticles to the transparent conducting electrode [57].

The photocurrent of the FTO/TiO2-C-150-30 electrode was much higher than the other electrodes with the following decreasing order: FTO/TiO2-C-150–30>FTO/TiO2-C-150–150>FTO/TiO2-C-120-150. Also, it is worth to mention that the highest photocurrent response of the C-TiO2-150-30 sample is related with a higher electroactive surface area of the electrode, which can be associated with the smallest particle size of the sample 0.29±0.06 compared with the others. Also, this sample presented lesser porosity than the others making the film less resistive in which the potential can extend longer inside it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric characterization of FTO/ZnO-C electrodes in the dark and upon light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 A, B and C shows the photocurrents obtained by cyclic voltammetry for the ZnO-C films. The CVs were carried out within a potential range from 1.5 to −0.5V vs. Ag/AgCl/NaCl 3M, at a potential scan rate, υ, of 20mVs−1 in 1M LiClO4 ethanol solution, in cathodic direction starting at EOCP. The current in the dark increases around −0.4V until the reverse potential is reached at −0.5V. Then, in the anodic direction, the current became very small again until the anodic current started to increase at potentials around 1.2V.

On the other hand, the voltammetric curves exhibited an increase in the current, especially in the anodic current, when the film is illuminated. In the case of the FTO/ZnO-C-150-30 electrode the photocurrent increased at around E=0.25V reaching a stationary value. The FTO/ZnO-C-150-150 electrode reached the stationary value at more positive potential, ca. E=0.5V.

In contrast, the FTO/ZnO-C-120-150 electrode did not reach an anodic current plateau and around 1.3V the current increased rapidly. In all the samples, when the potential scan is reversed to cathodic direction, the photocurrent kept the almost stationary value and decreased rapidly until the same value it had in the dark. The difference in the photocurrent generated by sample ZnO-C-150-30 is greater than those observed on ZnO-C-150-150 and ZnO-C-120-150 samples, even though they were prepared having approximately the same thickness of the deposited film onto the FTO electrode.

Fig. 10 shows an open circuit potential (OCP) variation in EOCP over time in the presence and absence of illumination for TiO2-C (Fig. 10A) and ZnO-C (Fig. 10B) films. The figure shows the time of initiating (On) and interrupting (Off) the illumination of FTO/TiO2-C and FTO/ZnO-C interfaces.

When both TiO2-C or ZnO-C films were illuminated by the FTO/TiO2-C or FTO/ZnO-C interface, electrons accumulate on either TiO2-C or ZnO-C and the EOCP turns more negative until the equilibrium is achieved reaching a stationary value; subsequently, upon interrupting illumination, the EOCP becomes less negative and varies asymptotically until reaching equilibrium EOCP in the dark.

Fig. 10A shows that under illumination, the EOCP of the electrodes with TiO2-C-150-30, TiO2-C-150-150, TiO2-C-120-150 films turns more negative than TiO2-C-120-30. This phenomenon can be related to the change of grain boundary surface states distribution, which can change at more negatives potentials, and the photogenerated electrons might get trapped in these energetic states tuning the Fermi level of TiO2 film causing the change observed in the photopotential in Fig. 10A [58]. The present results are in good agreement with sensitized TiO2-based photoelectrodes [29,30]. Zhang and co-workers [29] reported that TiO2-based photoelectrodes increased the photocurrent density from 0.017 to 0.28mAcm−2 after co-sensitized with CdS and PbS in the ITO/TiO2/CdS/PbS photoelectrode. This enhancement was attributed to a reduction in the energy band gap of the semiconductor. In addition, Peng and co-workers [30] reported an enhancement in the photoelectrochemistry response of TiO2 nanowires decorated with Ag nanoparticles [30]. This enhancement was attributed to the surface plasmon resonance of the noble metal which is responsible of the generation of hot electrons from metallic Ag nanoparticles at the interface between Ag and TiO2. As consequence, the Ag-decorated TiO2 nanowire photoanode displayed a fast response yielding 18μAcm−2 photocurrent density while TiO2 nanowire photoanode did not show any photoresponse to produce photocurrent under visible light illumination.

A similar behavior was observed for ZnO-C films. However, these samples showed a lesser negative change of their EOCP than TiO2-C-150-30, TiO2-C-150-150 and TiO2-C-120-150 films. The ZnO-C films presented more porosity and their particle size is smaller than the TiO2-C films, which can increase the recombination frequency of charge carriers in the porous matrix, decreasing the generated photocurrents of their voltammetric responses as showed above in Fig. 9. The micrograph of the ZnO-C-120-150 sample showed amorphous aggregates (Fig. 3B) compared with those from ZnO-C-150-30 (Fig. 3C) and ZnO-C-150-150 (Fig. 3D) which can contribute to decrease the photocurrent.

It has been reported by Han and co-workers [32] that a ternary CdS/ZnO/graphene composite developed a high-efficiency in the photoelectrochemistry response under solar irradiation. This ternary composite photoanode showed a very high photocurrent density, around 16.8mAcm−2 at 0.6 bias versus Ag/AgCl, which is much higher than the ZnO (2.43mAcm−2) Photoanode. These authors proposed a synergy effect between the three components of the photoanode to explain this behavior with the graphene playing the role of quantum-dots for the sensitization of ZnO in agreement with the present results. In addition, Zhao and co-workers [33] and Lv and co-workers [34] reported the influence of the decrease of the structural point defects upon the photoresponse of ZnO thin films [33] and ZnO nanorods [34]. These results support the fact that the present ZnO-based photoelectrodes showed a very-well defined crystalline zincite structure as discussed above (Fig. 5B).

Figs. S3 and S4 (Supplementary material) showed the photocurrent curves obtained in linear scan voltammograms performed on the TiO2-C and ZnO-C films. These photocurrents vs E curves were measured in a potential range in which a very small faradic process in the dark was observed, between −0.7 and 0.0V (vs. Ag/AgCl/NaCl 3M) in anodic direction. The photocurrent of these voltammograms, rises rapidly as the potential increases because the separation of (e−–h+) pairs is favored, and this phenomenon was observed in all films, except with TiO2-C-120-30, which showed small photocurrent of its cyclic voltammetric response and less EOCP variation as well. The TiO2-C-150-30 film presented the highest photocurrent consequence of its better photoelectrochemical behavior as discussed above.

When the ZnO-C films were illuminated, these electrodes exhibited a similar behavior to that seen in cyclic voltammetric curves. As well as TiO2-C films, the linear voltammograms of ZnO-C films, showed a rapidly increase in the photocurrent in anodic direction due to the separation of (e−–h+) pairs. This increase was observed up to the point where the separation rate of charge carriers becomes equal to the anodic reaction rate giving rise to a current plateau [59], which was observed in all films. For this reason, the photocurrent on the plateau of linear scan voltammograms can be interpreted as a measurement of photoelectrochemical activity of the films.

C-doped photoactive spherical TiO2 and ZnO semiconductors were prepared by microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis. The experimental reaction conditions of temperature and time of microwave-assisted synthesis were optimized and two C-doped TiO2 and ZnO samples were obtained with a clear red-shift in the energy band gap of the semiconductors, mainly in TiO2-based materials. The present results permit to conclude that the tailoring of the crystalline phase, morphology, and porosity characteristics can be achieved by microwave-assisted synthesis and therefore, this technique is a promising design tool for the preparation of C-doped mesopore photoactive semiconductors with a mixture of anatase and rutile phases. From the electrochemistry characterization of C-doped TiO2 and ZnO films can be concluded that the photocurrent obtained is an indicative of the photoelectrochemical activity of the films. The electrochemical characterization showed that TiO2-C and ZnO-C films are highly dependent on the conditions of synthesis. The TiO2-C-150-30 film presented higher photocurrent compared with the ZnO-C-150-30 film and can generate a larger EOCP difference. Therefore, it can be concluded that the microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis is an alternative, cheaper, and eco-friendly route to prepare materials with potential applications as photoelectrodes for quantum-dots sensitized solar cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to thank mainly to the Mexican CONACYT-SENER-Sustentabilidad Energética 2014-02 (245467) project, and to the Chilean Basal Program PFB-27 and FONDEF project: ID15i10321, for the funds to develop this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.10.236.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: www.ise.fraunhofer.de. (2014). Electricity generation from solar and wind energy in 2013.
